details.text:
مَا أَنْتَ بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ بِمَجْنُونٍ

- This is جَوابُ القَسْم (Jawaab ul-Qasm – Answer to the oath) for the oaths made in the previous verse i.e., “(I swear) By the pen and what they write, you are not by the favour of your Rabb (lord) insane”.

ن ۚ وَالْقَلَمِ وَمَا يَسْطُرُونَ

- That which is being made an oath upon (المُقْسَمُ بِه - Al-Muqsam Bihi) are two things 1) The pen and 2) Everything what they write down or inscribe.

Derivative: مَهِينٍ is an insulting word refers to someone who is contemptible and of little worth. It was used to refer to a camel that can’t impregnate a she-camel. It refers to someone of weak substance.

Derivative: حَلَّافٍ is in the مُبَالَغَة (Mubaalaghah – Hyperbolised) form and means the one who continuously swears upon things. A حَالِف (Haalif) is someone who swears an oath (حَلْف - Half) whilst حَلَّافٍ (Hallaaf) is the one who continuously swears oaths.
Derivative: هَمَّازٍ is in the مُبَالَغَة (Mubaalaghah – Hyperbolised) form and means the one who continuously says bad things about people, condescending and backbiting.
Derivative: نَمِيمٍ means to speak about people in a maliciously hurtful and harmful manner that would make them look bad and be offensive to them.
Derivative: مُعْتَدٍ is someone who transgresses the limits.
Derivative: أَثِيمٍ is in the فَعِيل (Fa’eel) form and means someone who is in a state of constant sinfulness.
Derivative: مَنَّاعٍ is in the مُبَالَغَة (Mubaalaghah – Hyperbolised) form and means the one who continuously prevents something.

Derivative: زَنِيمٍ can refer to a state of being humiliated or lowly. It can refer to a camel that was cut, branded or marked and known as مُزَنَّم (Muzannam) to indicate that it shouldn’t be slaughtered. It can refer to viewing oneself to be above criticism, untouchable and beyond reproach. It has also been said to mean an illegitimate pretender who claims to be from a people but is actually not from them.

Derivative: عُتُلٍّ is another kind of مُبَالَغَة (Mubaalaghah – Hyperbolised) form based on the فُعِلّ pattern. عَتَلَ means to be tough and rigid. عُتُلٍّ is someone who is very mean, cruel, harsh and extremely unlikeable.
Derivative: أَسَاطِيرُ is from أُسْطُورَة which means a legend.

Derivative: الْخُرْطُومِ refers to an elephant’s trunk and can refer to a person’s large nose. However, the real figurative meaning of خُرْطُومِ in Arabic literature is that they say خَراطِيم القَوْم (Kharaateem ul-Qawm) to refer to the most arrogant people of the nation, because they are stuck up and hold their noses up high which is associated with pride. As such the nose could be symbolic of pride and arrogance.
Derivative: مُصْبِحِينَ is a حَال (Haal – Description of a state or condition attached to an action) which means when they arise in the morning.
Derivative: يَصْرِمُ is from صَرُمَ which means to cut and refers to cutting fruit when it is being harvested.

Derivative: الصَّرِيم it is the فَعِيل pattern with the meaning of the مَفْعُول (object of the verb) i.e., the meaning of مَصْرُوم. It means to be cut or plucked from the roots which in respect to plants and trees means that they have been rendered useless and cannot be benefited from again.
Derivative: تَنَادَوْا means to call to each other from تَنَادَى – يَتَنادَى – تَنادِيًا.
Derivative: اغْدُوا from غَدَا – يَغْدُو – غَدْوًا means to go or set off in the morning. It means to go out at the first part of the day. اغْدُوا therefore means to set out early.
Derivative: يَتَخَافَتُونَ means to speak quietly with one another which implies not wanting speech to be heard by others.
Derivative: حَرْدٍ refers to anger and implies strong intent and determination. Here it refers to the strong intent to deter.
Derivative: مَحْرُومُونَ means to be deprived and dispossessed from حَرُمَ which means to deprive.

Derivative: مَمْنُونٍ comes from مَنَّ and has the same meaning as قَطَعَ which means “to cut”. مَمْنُونٍ (Mamnoon) means the same as مَقْطُوع (Maqtoo’) which means to be cut. Here, it means to be cut off or interrupted. غَيْرَ مَمْنُونٍ (Not Mamnoon) means uninterrupted, not cut off and continual i.e., never ending.
Derivative: يَتَلَاوَمُونَ from تَلاوَمَ – يَتَلَاوَمُ – تَلاوُمًا which means to blame one another.
- Is an expression related to the realisation of ruin of destruction having befallen them, when the worst thing possible or imagined has occurred.

Derivative: رَاغِبُونَ is from رَغِبَ. رَغِبَ إلَى means to be inclined towards something and رَغِبَ عَن means to be inclined or turned away from something. رَاغِبُونَ here is linked to إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا and means “We will only be inclined and turn towards our Rabb (Lord and Master) from now on”.
Derivative: النَّعِيمِ is a صِفَة (Sifah – Adjective) describing the gardens of paradise (Jannaat) and means “of permanent luxury and blessing”.
Derivative: خُلُقٍ (Khuluq) refers to how someone deals and interacts with the خَلْق (Khalq i.e., creation). خُلُقٍ refers to noble and honourable characteristics, morals and manners.
Derivative: تَرْهَقُ from رَهِق which means to pile burdens, one on top of another. رَهَق (Rahaq) refers to layers of burdens. Here it means to be overcome and covered by.
Derivative: سَالِمُونَ means to be well and sound.

Derivative: نَسْتَدْرِجُ from اِسْتَدْرَجَ – اِسْتِدْراج which means to slowly or progressively take someone up the ranks and refers to enticing someone bit by bit to what will in the end be their own ruin.
Derivative: مَتِينٌ is something that is extremely firm and strong. A rope that is مَتِينٌ (Mateen) is one that can’t be cut. In this context it means unescapable.

- لِحُكْمِ – The لَام (Laam - لِ) here is called لَام التَوْقيت (Laam ut-Tawqeet) which is the Laam that relates to timing or the reaching of a deadline. Its meaning here is “until”. لِحُكْمِ therefore means “until the judgement”.
Derivative: الْعَرَاءِ means naked and bare. الْعَرَاءِ refers to an open bare place and implies a desolate barren place.
Derivative: اجْتَبَى means to chose or select someone based on qualities that have been observed in that person.

Derivative: يُزْلِقُونَ from أَزْلَقَ – يُزْلِقُ – إِزْلاق and means to cause to slip and be jolted from his place. When used with بَصَر (sight) it means to stare with such vehemence and hatred that it can shake someone from his place and make him fall down to the ground i.e., a stare that carries a desire for death and ruin towards the one who it is being directed towards.
Derivative: الْمَفْتُونُ refers to someone who loses their mind because of incidents. It also refers to someone who has been stricken by an affliction, trial or enchantment.

Derivative: تُدْهِنُ from أَدْهَنَ – يُدْهِنُ – إِدْهان. It originates from دُهْن (Duhn) which is oil and إِدْهان means to oil or grease something to make it more pliable and flexible. It is used to refer to softening someone’s position so that he makes compromises. أَدْهَنَ can also imply speaking to someone softly despite your hatred towards him i.e., to make him feel that he is your friend in an act of deception for insincere motives.
